coredata.dc:title: Cerium-mediated photooxidation for tuning pH-dependent oxidase-like activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijia
Pang, Ernan
Gao, Chao
Chang, Qing
Hu, Shengliang
Li, Ning
coredata.dc:description: Industrial and medical applications of most nanozymes are often limited by their pH-dependent activities as well as by weak response to external stimuli. Here we report a light-controlled nanozyme possessing tunable oxidase-like activities without incurring the burden of pH effects. This nanozyme comprises carbon dots (CDs) and Ce3+ ions and its activities can be regulated with rational design of their concentration ratio. Unlike previously reported nanozymes, yet it can perform optimally at neutral pH values for catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). We find that its unique attribute originates from pH-dependent ability of CDs to reversibly switch from Ce3+ to Ce4+ under visible light irradiation. Therefore, our presented nanozyme can be applied in real environment and be simultaneously remotely-controlled by visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Photooxidation
Catalytic oxidation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of TMB solution (0.8 mM) oxidized by CDs (15 mg/L) under different conditions at pH 4.0; (b) ESR spectra from samples containing 100 mM TEMP and 15 mg/L CDs in different pH buffer solution; (c) Photos of TMB solution color evolution via CDs oxidation in the presence or absence of Ce3+ (3.2 mM) and EDTA-2Na (3.2 mM) at different pH values after reaction for 5 min with or without visible light irradiation; (d) The dependence of the absorbance at 652 nm from different samples on pH values; (e) and (f) The effects of the concentrations of CDs or Ce3+ ions in the presence of 3.2 mM EDTA-2Na on TMB oxidation at pH 4.0 and pH 7.0 with visible light irradiation for 5 min.

(a) Steady-state kinetics for oxidase-like activity of CDs with visible light irradiation at pH 4.0; (b) and (c) Steady-state kinetics for oxidase-like activity of the mixed system of CDs (15 mg/L) and Ce3+ ions (0.8 mM) with visible light irradiation at pH 4.0 and pH 7.0; (d) The effects of pH values on zeta potentials of CDs in the presence or absence of Ce3+ and EDTA-2Na; (e) The intensity changes of PL emission peak at 460 nm from CDs in the presence or absence of Ce3+ and EDTA-2Na with pH values under 365 nm excitation; (f) Time-resolved PL spectra of CDs in the presence and absence of Ce3+ and EDTA-2Na at different pH values.

(a) Absorption spectra of Ce4+, CDs solution and the mixed system of Ce3+ ions and CDs with different irradiation time at pH 4.0; (b) Absorption spectra of CDs and the mixed system of Ce3+ ions and CDs with different irradiation time at pH 7.0; (c) and (d) Cyclic voltammograms of the mixed system of Ce3+ ions (3.2 mM) and EDTA-2Na (3.2 mM) in buffer solution of pH 4.0 and 7.0 at scan rate of 50 mV s−1, respectively.
Schematic for the mechanism of Ce3+-boosted oxidase-like activity of CDs with visible light irradiation at different pH values.

head.ce:title: Cerium-mediated photooxidation for tuning pH-dependent oxidase-like activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial and medical applications of most nanozymes are often limited by their pH-dependent activities as well as by weak response to external stimuli. Here we report a light-controlled nanozyme possessing tunable oxidase-like activities without incurring the burden of pH effects. This nanozyme comprises carbon dots (CDs) and Ce3+ ions and its activities can be regulated with rational design of their concentration ratio. Unlike previously reported nanozymes, yet it can perform optimally at neutral pH values for catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). We find that its unique attribute originates from pH-dependent ability of CDs to reversibly switch from Ce3+ to Ce4+ under visible light irradiation. Therefore, our presented nanozyme can be applied in real environment and be simultaneously remotely-controlled by visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: Funtional nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking activities, defined as nanozymes, are currently attracting considerable interest owing to their unique advantages over natural enzymes, e.g. low cost, durability, robustness to harsh environments, ease of mass production and storage [1,2]. Since the high activity is one of the prerequisites for their applications [3–5], many related strategies have been developed including size and morphology regulation, composition design as well as metal ion modifications [6–10]. Nevertheless, most peroxidase- and oxidase-like nanozymes show obviously pH-dependent activities for chromogenic substrates, e.g. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), which is widely employed as a typical horseradish peroxidase substrate in various bioassays [11,12]. In general, the ability of nanozymes to oxidize TMB decreases dramatically as the pH value increases from 4.0 to 7.0 [9,12–14]. At pH 7.0, many nanozymes even lose their activities completely to its oxidation. For instance, Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibited maximum peroxidase-like activity at pH 3.5 but full deactivation at pH 7.0 for TMB [11]. Similarly, Nitrogen-doped porous carbon nanospheres with multienzyme mimetic activity also displayed the pH-dependent capability to oxidize TMB, with maximum activity at pH 4.5 and no activity at pH 7.0 [4]. CeO2 behaves as an oxidase-like nanozyme in a pH-dependent manner and performs optimally at pH 4.0 [15]. Therefore, the efficient oxidation of TMB at neutral pH through nanozymes is still a challenge.

Photo-oxidase nanozymes have an advantage in remotely-controlled activities using light, beneficial to in situ operation of the catalytic reaction. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) possess superior singlet oxygen (1O2) generation capability, and can be used as photo-oxidase nanozymes [16,17]. However, CDs are difficult to efficiently oxidize TMB at neutral pH with illumination. To break their pH limitation of enzyme-mimicking activity, Zhang et al. reported that Mn ion-mediated CDs can facilitate TMB oxidation at neutral pH under 365 nm irradiation [10]. Unfortunately, the optimal activity of this system still occurs at acidic pH. Herein, we find that Ce ions show a novel function distinguishing from Mn ions. Not only can they help CDs to break the pH limitation in photooxidation stimulated with visible light, but also induce optimal activity at neutral pH. Meanwhile, we also confirm that this case results from pH-dependent synergistic effect among CDs and Ce ions for regulating photooxidation activity.

We used amino-functionalized CDs (Figs. S1 and S2), which were obtained by the reported method [18,19], for photooxidation of TMB. Typically, Fig. 1 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB solution at pH 4.0 in the presence of CDs. Without light irradiation, TMB cannot be oxidized by CDs (Blue line in Fig. 1a). Under visible light irradiation (100 mW·cm−2), CDs enable TMB oxidation within minutes in absence of H2O2, producing a product (TMBox) with blue color that has a characteristic absorbance at ~652 nm (Red line in Fig. 1a). However, the characteristic absorption peak belonging to TMBox dramatically decreases as the N2 is pumped into TMB reaction solution (Green line in Fig. 1a). This phenomenon verifies that O2 is necessary for TMB oxidation. According to previous reports, the CDs can produce 1O2 with a relatively high quantum yield that can directly oxidize TMB at acidic pH values [10,17]. By electron spin resonance (ESR) technique, we attemped to trap 1O2 on the basis of the report [20]. As shown in Fig. 1b, the characteristic 1O2-induced signals are presented in the ESR spectra merely under visible light irradiation (for 10 min) and they cannot be influenced with pH variation. This confirms the generation of 1O2 via light irradiation of CDs, thereby causing TMB oxidation in the presence of CDs.

According to contrast experiments (Top and bottom rows in Fig. 1c, Fig. S3), TMB can’t be oxidized in the presence of oxygen without CDs and light irradiation at any pH values. This indicates that the oxidation of TMB to TMBox is specific to CDs excited with visible light. As for mere CDs, their catalytic activity toward the oxidation of TMB reduces with the increment of the pH value (Middle rows in Fig. 1c, Blue line in Fig. 1d), consistent with the previous observations [13,21]. The mixed system of Ce3+ ions and CDs exhibits the higher activity than CDs alone, and it starts to oxidize TMB into blue color at the higher pH (Middle rows in Fig. 1c). But its activity toward the oxidation of TMB still declines with pH increase as depicted in Fig. 1d (Green line). Interestingly, the oxidation rate of TMB is significantly boosted from acidic to neutral pH values as the complexing agent of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) is simultaneously added into the system with Ce3+ ions (Middle rows in Fig. 1c). The characteristic absorbance of TMBox continuously increases from pH 3.0 to 7.0 as well (Red line in Fig. 1d). In particular, the maximum absorbance is present at pH 7.0, surpassing that at acidic pH. This amazing phenomenon is firstly observed till now at least what we know.

Furthermore, we explored the effects of the concentrations of CDs or Ce3+ ions on TMB oxidation under different pH conditions. At pH 4.0, the ability of the mixed system of CDs, EDTA-2Na and Ce3+ ions (named as CS for easy description) to oxidize TMB is unchanged as the Ce3+ concentration increases, whereas it can be linearly enhanced with increasing CDs concentration (Fig. 1e). At pH 7.0, the CS ability exhibits a good linear dependence on the concentrations of CDs in the case of excess Ce3+ ions (Fig. 1f). Differently, the CS photooxidation ability is firstly enhanced with more Ce3+ addition and then becomes almost invariable at the given concentration of CDs (Fig. 1f). The response limit of Ce3+ was calculated to be 26 nM based on 3σ/m (σ, the standard deviation; m, the slope between signal intensity and Ce3+ concentration, Fig. S4). All of above results indicate that the cooperative effect among CDs and Ce3+ ions for TMB oxidation is determined by their concentration ratio and pH values under visible light irradiation.

For our presented nanozymes, the steady-state kinetic parameters of oxidase-like reaction at different pH values were also determined. From Fig. 2 a-c, typical Michaelis–Menten curves are observed for CDs and CS at different pH values. Results indicate that the apparent K m value reduces with the addition of Ce3+ and pH increase, implying that the CS has a higher affinity for TMB at pH 7.0 than pH 4 as well as only CDs. Table S1 provides the comparisons of pH-dependent activities of our CS with other nanozymes reported in the literatures. The activities of most reported nanozymes at pH 7.0 are less than half that at pH 4.0, whereas our CS at pH 7.0 shows a level of activity 125% times higher than that at pH 4.0. Accordingly, the addition of Ce3+ ions can efficiently improve the photo-oxidase ability of CDs at neutral condition.

Fig. 2d shows that the surface potential of CDs is negative and varies with pH slightly. After adding Ce3+ ions, the degree of electronegativity reduction is almost constant in acidic pH ranges, whereas the potential of CDs becomes positive when pH value is close to neutral condition. Correspondingly, the electrostatic interaction among CDs and Ce3+ ions is boosted at neutral condition. From Fig. 2d, their interaction is greatly lessened by complexing Ce3+ to EDTA-2Na since the probability of direct contact among CDs and Ce3+ ions declines. In addition, we measured the photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different pH values before and after adding Ce3+ ions without changing the concentration of CDs (Fig. S5). From Fig. 2e, the PL emission intensity of CDs gradually decreases from pH 4.0 to 7.0 after adding CDs. This result certifies that the photoinduced charge transfer from CDs to Ce3+ ions (which cannot absorb visible light) depends on pH values. The average lifetime of CDs in the absence of Ce3+ ions suffers from attenuation with pH 4.0 condition (Fig. 2f and Fig. S6). The addition of Ce3+ ions enables PL lifetime decay of CDs in acidic pH ranges, but dramatically promotes PL lifetime of CDs while the condition is close to neutral pH values. By complex reaction with cerium species, one can find that EDTA-2Na plays a role in relieving PL lifetime decay of CDs in the presence of Ce3+ ions (Fig. 2f). Generally, the electron transfer is faster than the radiation recombination process and enables the reduction of the average lifetime. As noted above, Ce3+ ions markedly enhance the lifetime of photoinduced electrons from CDs at neutral pH. Therefore, we infer that the photoinduced holes could be transferred into electronorbitals of Ce3+ ions, possibly making the electrons on excited states longer owning to lack of holes. Meanwhile, this transfer process may lead to the oxidation of Ce3+ ions.

Furthermore, we monitored the mixed system of CDs and Ce3+ ions using UV–vis spectrophotometer to determine if Ce4+ ions can be generated under visible light irradiation. At pH 4.0, the buffer solution of Ce3+ ions (CeCl3·7H2O) displays a typical absorption peak at 270 nm while that of Ce4+ ions (Ce(SO4)2) has a broad wavelength absorption ranging from 250 to 400 nm. After adding CDs, no new peak comes out in UV–Vis spectra of Ce3+ buffer solution. As expected, the absorbance of the mixed system of CDs and Ce3+ ions is changeless with visible light irradiation (Fig. 3 a). At pH 7.0, Ce4 ions in buffer solution display a broad characteristic absorption peak centred at around 300 nm. Under visible light irradiation, the mixed system of CDs and Ce3+ ions also produce new absorbance in typical wavelength ranges belonging to Ce4+ absorption at pH 7.0, and its absorbance gradually increases with irradiation time (Fig. 3b). Therefore, these results confirm that Ce3+ ions can be oxidized to Ce4+ ions in the presence of CDs under visible light irradiation. Additionally, we determined the redox potentials of Ce3+/Ce4+ at different pH values in the presence of EDTA-2Na through cyclic voltammetry. Specifically, the reversible redox peak appears around +0.85 V versus Ag/AgCl at pH 4.0 (Fig. 3c) while it locates at +0.94 V versus Ag/AgCl at pH 7.0 (Fig. 3d), which is consistent with previous reports as well [22]. Clearly, the redox potential of Ce3+/Ce4+ relies on pH value, thus making it easy to match the energy level of CDs via pH variations. As for EDTA-2Na, it could offer ligands for stabilizing Ce4+ ions to avoid the formation of cerium hydroxide. On the other hand, it could help the holes transfer from CDs to Ce3+ ions since it commonly serves as a hole scavenger, thus promoting the oxidation of Ce3+ ions.

Fig. 4 illustrates the mechanism of Ce3+-boosted oxidase-like activity of CDs. At acidic pH, 1O2 generated from CDs plays a key role in TMB oxidation under illumination. As pH increases, the potentials of Ce3+/Ce4+ gradually decrease and the photoinduced hole transfers from CDs to Ce3+ ions are available to facilitate Ce3+ oxidation. Alternatively, 1O2 may be endowed with an ability to oxidize Ce3+ at neutral pH values though it is difficult to enable TMB oxidation. There are three evidences. Firstly, the absorbance at 652 nm decreased when N2 was pumped into the CS in neutral conditions, as was observed at acidic pH (Fig. S7). Secondly, we did not detect other reactive oxygen species (such as
O2 − and
OH) in the reaction process (Fig. S8). Thirdly, the CS ability to TMB oxidation decreased apparently in the presence of 1O2 scavengers (Fig. S9). Consequently, the high valence Ce4+ ions formed by Ce3+ oxidation is likely to be a core contributor for photooxidation of TMB at neutral pH. This is also supported by the above fact that merely neutral pH condition gives rise to more TMBox production with more Ce3+ ions added (Fig. 1f). Yet, it should be noted that the CDs act as an integral componentof implementing photooxidation process. In absence of CDs, only Ce3+ ions is difficult to form stable Ce4+ ions and oxidize TMB under illumination (Fig. S10). Thereby, the optimal oxidase-like activity of the combination of CDs with Ce3+ ions occurred at neutral pH values is attributed to their synergetic effect in the conversion of Ce3+ to Ce4+ using visible light energy.

By the combination of CDs with Ce3+ ions, we obtained a light stimuli-responsive nanozyme with highly efficient oxidase-like activities for TMB oxidation. The oxidation potentials of Ce3+/Ce4+ and the charge transfers from CDs to Ce3+ ions are significantly dependent on pH values. Accordingly, the synergetic effect of CDs and Ce3+ ions in the conversion of Ce3+ to Ce4+ using visible light energy gradually works as pH increases. Meanwhile, EDTA-2Na could play the crucial roles in promoting the hole transfer from CDs to Ce3+ ions to produce more Ce4+ ions and in stabilizing them. Not only does our presented nanozyme perform optimally at neutral pH values, but also its activities can be regulated with rational design of the concentration ratio of both components.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082, 201803D421091), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China; and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1510125, 51502270, 21703209), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.125471.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
